detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does wealth determine the characters' fates in âThe Great Gatsbyâ?

subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: Clear articulation of topic focus on wealth and fate
Well-stated, narrow research question linked tightly to analysis
Methodology identifies primary and secondary sources with close-reading approach
weaknesses: Methodology lacks systematic rationale for source selection and critical comparison
Occasional informal phrasing (âI am going to elaborate onâ) breaks academic tone
Introduction includes overly broad contextual summary that dilutes focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates topic and research question with clarity and appropriateness (band 5), but the methodological description is only partially complete (band 3). On balance, performance sits in the upper half of the 3â4 band, meriting a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Relevant application of primary quotations to support theme analyses
Clear knowledge of plot, character, and historical context
Mostly accurate use of core literary concepts (American Dream, symbolism)
weaknesses: Reliance on generic paraphrase and study-guide commentary limits depth
Technical terminology usage is inconsistent and occasionally informal
Analytical integration of secondary material remains descriptive rather than evaluative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Subject knowledge and application of source material are coherent but with descriptive integration (band 3â4), while terminology use is mostly accurate but not consistently precise (band 3). Overall performance aligns with a mid-band score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Primary text analysis consistently relevant to the research question
Sustained line of argument with generally effective support of conclusions
Insightful evaluation of moral and social implications of wealth
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is surface-level at times, with limited counter-interpretation
Research quality is adequate but could be broadened with more scholarly sources
Minor inconsistencies in linking historical context and secondary material
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research relevance is solid (band 4â6), analysis is sustained though not deeply probing (band 4â6), and discussion offers coherent argument with some inconsistencies (band 4â6). The work fits comfortably in the lower half of the 4â6 band, meriting 5 out of 12.

strengths: Structure is complete and appropriate, with clear headings and table of contents
Layout includes required elements (title, word count, bibliography)
Headings effectively guide reader through thematic sections
weaknesses: Pagination in contents list does not always match actual pages
Occasional OCR artifacts and long sentences reduce readability
Some paragraph breaks and sentence completions need attention
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essayâs structure and layout meet top-band expectations (band 3â4) but exhibit minor formatting and readability issues. This performance corresponds to a score of 3 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â âTo what extent does wealth determine the charactersâ fates in The Great Gatsby?â â is **clearly stated, sufficiently narrow, and firmly linked** to the ensuing analysis.  Each section (American Dream, Old vs New Money, Consequences) is explicitly tied back to this question, showing effective connection.  Because occasional digressions (e.g., lengthy summary of Jazz Age context) slightly dilute focus, the strand earns 5 rather than 6.

The essay moves beyond summary and offers **reasonably sustained analysis** (e.g., linking Gatsbyâs bootlegging to moral decay), but arguments often stay surface-level, with limited interrogation of counter-interpretations.  Occasional repetition reduces depth, so the lower end of the 4-6 band (score 4) is appropriate.

Quotations from the novel are used appropriately to illustrate points about wealth, and the few secondary references are relevant, but integration is often descriptive rather than analytical.  **Most material is connected to the argument**, yet reliance on generic paraphrase and study-guide commentary limits sophistication, meriting a mid-band score of 3.

Required layout elementsâtitle, session, word count, and a bibliographyâare present, and basic formatting is consistent.  Although the extract does not show page numbers or formal citation style throughout, the general presentation meets expectations sufficiently for a top-band score of 3.

The student demonstrates **clear knowledge of plot, character, and historical context** and can relate these to themes of the American Dream and class.  While some points are repetitive, the discussion shows coherent understanding, and sources are used with partial effectiveness.  This aligns with the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The essay follows the expected EE structure (title page, contents, intro, analytical sections, conclusion, bibliography).  Headings guide the reader, but pagination in the contents does not always match the excerpt, and paragraphing is occasionally lengthy.  Overall **structure is appropriate and mostly complete**, warranting 3 in the top presentation band.

Research is **adequate and generally relevant**: primary text analysis is sustained and the few secondary sources support class/wealth themes.  Breadth is modest, yet selection aligns with the question without major gaps.  This positions the work solidly in the 4-6 band; a 5 reflects a competent but not wide-ranging evidence base.

The student clearly states the focus on how wealth influences fate in The Great Gatsby in both the title and opening paragraph, and the purpose (to assess Fitzgeraldâs critique of the American Dream) is reiterated throughout the introduction and conclusion.  The scope is limited to the main characters and to textual evidence from the novel, making the topic **appropriate, focused and consistently communicated**.  Minor vagueness remains in phrasing such as âgoing to elaborate on,â so a secure but not perfect 5 is appropriate within the top band.

A line of argument is present from introduction to conclusion, and claims are generally supported by textual references.  **Evaluation of implications** (e.g., moral limits of the American Dream) appears, yet reasoning can be sweeping and lacks nuanced weighing of alternative views.  Minor inconsistencies place the discussion at a competent but not highly sophisticated level, hence 4.

The methodology is **partially complete**: the essay identifies primary (the novel) and a few secondary sources, and explains that it will rely on close reading.  However, there is little evidence of systematic source selection or critical comparison of scholarly views; only three secondary items (two online study guides) are cited.  No rationale for criteria of inclusion or research process is provided, so the work fits comfortably in the 3-4 band, low side.

Literary concepts such as symbolism, âold money vs new money,â and âAmerican Dreamâ are invoked accurately, but technical terminology (e.g., narrative voice, motif) is limited and sometimes informal (âtwisted tale,â âvapidâ).  Usage is **mostly accurate but not consistently precise**, fitting the middle band.

annotations.comment: The research question is succinctly stated and tightly linked to the investigationâs focus on wealth and fate in The Great Gatsby, providing a clear and effective anchor for the essay.
The word count is provided and falls just under the maximum limit, fulfilling the formatting requirement.
No clear investigative title appears at the start. Adding a concise title reflecting the focus (e.g., âWealth and Fate in The Great Gatsbyâ) would strengthen the reportâs formatting.
Including a Table of Contents demonstrates sound structure and guides the reader effectively through the sections.
Page numbering does not appear on the page footers, making it difficult to match the contents list to actual pages. Ensure consistent pagination.
The âTable of Contents:â heading is clearly labeled, supporting navigability and presentation.
The opening paragraph contains substantial summary. Tighten the introduction to focus more directly on the research question.
The methodology statement clearly identifies reliance on close reading of the primary text, showing an informed method for literary analysis.
Phrasing such as âI am going to elaborate onâ is informal and shifts into future tense. Rephrase to maintain academic tone and immediacy.
Describing Fitzgeraldâs plot as a âtwisted taleâ is colloquial. Replace with more precise literary terminology like âcomplex narrative.â
The analysis of Gatsbyâs pursuit of wealth is well supported by textual references and connects directly to your argument on moral limits.
Employment of Daisyâs character analysis effectively applies novel quotations to illustrate the theme of wealth and its hollow effects.
Myrtle Wilsonâs tragic arc is portrayed with strong use of primary text evidence, reinforcing the essayâs central theme.
The portrayal of Nick Carraway demonstrates clear subject knowledge and situates his narrative function effectively within thematic analysis.
The âThe US Analysis and Critiqueâ heading clearly organizes the analytical sections, aiding reader navigation.
The historical context paragraph relies on only one primary source. Integrate additional scholarly articles to deepen contextual research quality.
This sentence breaks off abruptly. Review paragraph continuations to ensure all sentences are complete and coherent.
The opening quotation is deployed effectively and demonstrates solid application of primary material.
Expand on the significance of this quotation by linking it more explicitly to character motivations and broader themes.
Your analysis of the Valley of Ashes symbol is insightful and demonstrates coherent interpretation of its thematic role.
The footnote citation format is inconsistent (extra HTML tags). Revise to match a single, uniform citation style.
Minor OCR artifacts appear (âmatical intenseâ). Proofread to correct garbled text and enhance clarity.
The connection between primary text outcomes and critique of the American Dream is articulated clearly.
Transitions between discussion of the novel and the secondary article are abrupt. Introduce smoother linking sentences.
âThe Pursuit of Wealthâ subheading guides the reader effectively through thematic sections.
Integration of Gatsbyâs line âHer voice is full of moneyâ is effective, reinforcing your analysis of his motivations.
Conclude this paragraph more fully by summarizing how each characterâs pursuit leads to distinct moral compromises.
The âThe Influence of Old Money vs. New Moneyâ heading delineates theme clearly and strengthens presentation.
Several paragraphs rely on lengthy quotations and summary. Paraphrase more concisely to maintain analytic focus.
LitCharts is a general reference; supplement with peer-reviewed scholarship to strengthen research quality.
The âConsequences of Wealthâ heading provides a clear structural demarcation for closing discussion.
The evaluation of personal and social consequences of wealth is incisive and supports your central line of argument.
Several sentences here exceed a single line. Break them into shorter sentences to enhance readability.
The extension suggesting future research on gender and race demonstrates evaluative reflection beyond the core analysis.
The conclusion repeats phrasing (âthe novel is an eternal reflectionâ). Vary language to avoid redundancy in closing remarks.
The bibliography is clearly labeled and follows expected structure.
The first bibliography entry lacks consistent access date formatting and publisher details. Standardize citation style.
Include publisher location or full URL for the third entry to meet comprehensive formatting standards.
